food_name: Lo Bak Go (Pan-fried Radish Cake, Dim Sum Version)

local_names.hindi: लो बाक गो (तली हुई मूली केक)

hindi: लो बाक गो (तली हुई मूली केक)

region_of_origin: Guangdong (Canton), China; widely adopted in Singapore and Malaysia
food_subgroup: Pan-fried steamed daikon radish and rice flour cake with dried shrimp and Chinese waxed sausage (lap cheong)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Lo Bak Go. The closest parent concept is a steamed-then-pan-fried starchy savoury cake made from root vegetable and rice flour. No URI is assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pan-fried steamed daikon and rice flour cake with dried shrimp and Chinese waxed sausage (lo bak go, dim sum style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CEB is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for mixed rice-flour-based savoury cakes. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for lo bak go specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for lo bak go or an equivalent steamed-then-pan-fried daikon rice flour cake.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dim sum serving of lo bak go is 3 pan-fried slices, weighing approximately 150 g, typically served on a small plate with sweet soy sauce and chilli sauce for dipping.

nutrient_source.title: Lo Bak Go (Pan-fried Radish Cake) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values per 100 g are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Lo Bak Go (pan-fried radish cake). This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Core macros and secondary nutrients are accepted at confidence 1.0 as verified. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9 and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values for comparable rice-flour-based dim sum items.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Rice flour and analogous starchy foods; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lo bak go in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: rice flour-based steamed and pan-fried cakes. Rice flour has a published GI of approximately 65–70 (University of Sydney GI Database). The high water and dietary fibre content of daikon radish moderates the effective GI downward to approximately 62 (medium). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (62 × 21.5 × 1.5) / 100 = approximately 20.0 g. This is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dried shrimp, lap cheong, and soy seasoning — 420 mg per 100 g, delivering approximately 630 mg per 150 g serving, before dipping sauces
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit lo bak go to one serving (3 slices, 150 g) per meal, avoid the sweet soy and chilli dipping sauces which can add 150–300 mg additional sodium, and monitor total meal sodium carefully when ordering multiple dim sum items.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 62) and moderate glycemic load (GL 20 per 150 g serving) driven by refined rice flour base delivering approximately 32 g carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit lo bak go to one serving (3 slices, 150 g) and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or protein-rich dim sum items to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the pan-frying oil and protein from dried shrimp and lap cheong provide some glycaemic moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (420 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (6.5 g/100 g) from pan-frying oil and lap cheong, with low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) and zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should restrict lo bak go to one serving per meal due to the high sodium load, choose vegetable oil pan-frying over lard where possible, and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces; the low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are favourable, but the sodium burden remains the primary cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Daikon radish provides vitamin C (8 mg/100 g) and anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; dried shrimp contributes selenium (8 mcg/100 g); low saturated fat profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lo bak go is generally acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; the anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile from daikon and selenium from dried shrimp is beneficial, though sodium intake should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lo Bak Go (蘿蔔糕, Radish Cake) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish and a staple of Lunar New Year celebrations. It is a dense, savoury cake made from grated daikon (white radish, lo bak) mixed into a seasoned rice flour batter together with dried shrimp, Chinese waxed sausage (lap cheong), shiitake mushrooms, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. The mixture is poured into a tray and steamed until firm, then cooled, sliced into rectangular pieces approximately 1–2 cm thick, and pan-fried in vegetable oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crisp while the interior remains soft, moist, and tender. The radish texture dissolves almost entirely into the rice flour matrix during steaming, producing a cohesive, slightly chewy interior. The flavour is distinctively savoury and umami-rich from the dried shrimp, with a subtle sweetness from the lap cheong. At dim sum restaurants it is served with sweet soy sauce and chilli sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, a 150 g serving (3 slices) provides approximately 237 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 9.8 g fat, and 32.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 420 mg per 100 g (630 mg per serving), making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving, requiring portion control for type 2 diabetes management. Despite being called 'carrot cake' by some Singaporeans — a misnomer arising from the Cantonese word for radish — it contains no carrots. It should be distinguished from hawker-style Chai Tow Kueh, which stir-fries the same base cake with egg and dark or white sauce.
common_user_queries: lo bak go vs chai tow kueh

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes) at confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA SR Legacy analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice flour as the closest defensible anchor, clearly documented. Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent with an honest gap note. All multilingual naming requirements for a Singaporean dish are satisfied.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo source, consistent with vegetable oil pan-frying. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown is not reported by the primary source and is set to -1.0. Water content is not reported by the primary source and is set to -1.0. GI value of 62 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for lo bak go is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. The FoodEx2 code A0CEB is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact code exists for this dish. FoodOn URI is null as no dedicated FoodOn concept exists for lo bak go. The duplicate portion entries in the input (two identical '1 serving (150g)' records) have been consolidated to a single canonical 150 g serving. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Foodo portal homepage; a direct deep-link to the specific food entry was not available in the input record. Hindi alias is a descriptive transliteration ('लो बाक गो — तली हुई मूली केक') as no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish. All entries present in aliases_by_language are also present in the flat aliases list to ensure full consistency.
